origin.question:
In general relativity, the Landau-Lifshitz stress-energy pseudotensor $t^{\mu
u}_{m LL}$ allows the definition of a conserved total stress-energy complex $H^{\mu
u} = (-g)(T^{\mu
u} + t^{\mu
u}_{m LL})$, satisfying the conservation law $\partial_\mu H^{\mu
u} = 0$. Consider the 'nearly Newtonian' metric $\mathrm{d}s^2 = -(1+2\Phi)\mathrm{d}t^2 + (1-2\Phi)\delta_{ij}\mathrm{d}x^i\mathrm{d}x^j$, where $\Phi \ll 1$ is the Newtonian gravitational potential and time derivatives of $\Phi$ are neglected compared to spatial derivatives. In this limit, the lowest nonvanishing order of the pseudotensor components are $t^{00}_{m LL} =  rac{1}{8\pi} |
abla \Phi|^2$ and $t^{ij}_{m LL} =  rac{1}{4\pi} \left( \Phi_{,i}\Phi_{,j} -  rac{1}{2}\delta_{ij} \Phi_{,k}\Phi_{,k}ight)$, with $t^{0i}_{m LL} = 0$. For a perfect fluid described by $T^{\mu
u} = (ho+p)u^\mu u^
u + p g^{\mu
u}$, prove that taking the Newtonian limit ($v \ll c$, $p \ll ho$) of the spatial conservation law $\partial_\mu H^{\mu i} = 0$ reduces exactly to the classical Euler equation of hydrodynamics: $ho  rac{d v^i}{dt} = -\partial_i p - ho \partial_i \Phi$.
origin.solution:
1. Expand the total stress-energy complex $H^{\mu
u} = (-g)(T^{\mu
u} + t^{\mu
u}_{m LL})$. For the given metric, $-g \approx 1 - 4\Phi \approx 1$ to lowest order, so $H^{\mu
u} \approx T^{\mu
u} + t^{\mu
u}_{m LL}$.2. Expand the perfect fluid stress-energy tensor $T^{\mu
u} = (ho+p)u^\mu u^
u + p g^{\mu
u}$ in the Newtonian limit ($v \ll c$, $p \ll ho$). With $u^0 \approx 1$ and $u^i \approx v^i$, we get $T^{00} \approx ho$, $T^{0i} \approx ho v^i$, and $T^{ij} \approx ho v^i v^j + p \delta^{ij}$.3. The spatial conservation law is $\partial_0 H^{0i} + \partial_j H^{ji} = 0$. Substituting the expanded components gives $\partial_0 (ho v^i) + \partial_j (ho v^j v^i + p \delta^{ji} + t^{ji}_{m LL}) = 0$.4. The derivative of the matter terms expands as $\partial_0 (ho v^i) + \partial_j (ho v^j v^i) = ho (\partial_0 v^i + v^j \partial_j v^i) + v^i (\partial_0 ho + \partial_j (ho v^j))$.5. Use the time-component conservation law $\partial_\mu H^{\mu 0} = 0$ to find the continuity equation. $H^{00} \approx ho + t^{00}_{m LL}$ and $H^{j0} \approx ho v^j$. Since time derivatives of $\Phi$ are neglected, $\partial_0 t^{00}_{m LL} \approx 0$, yielding $\partial_0 ho + \partial_j (ho v^j) = 0$. This makes the $v^i$ term vanish, simplifying the matter derivative to $ho  rac{d v^i}{dt}$.6. The spatial conservation law becomes $ho  rac{d v^i}{dt} + \partial_i p + \partial_j t^{ji}_{m LL} = 0$.7. Calculate the divergence of the pseudotensor: $\partial_j t^{ji}_{m LL} = \partial_j \left[  rac{1}{4\pi} (\Phi_{,j}\Phi_{,i} -  rac{1}{2}\delta_{ij} \Phi_{,k}\Phi_{,k})ight] =  rac{1}{4\pi} (\Phi_{,jj}\Phi_{,i} + \Phi_{,j}\Phi_{,ij}) -  rac{1}{4\pi} \Phi_{,k}\Phi_{,ki}$.8. The second and third terms cancel (by relabeling dummy indices $j 	o k$), leaving $\partial_j t^{ji}_{m LL} =  rac{1}{4\pi} \Phi_{,i} 
abla^2 \Phi$.9. In the Newtonian limit, the Einstein field equations reduce to the Poisson equation $
abla^2 \Phi = 4\pi ho$.10. Substituting the Poisson equation gives $\partial_j t^{ji}_{m LL} = ho \Phi_{,i}$.11. Substituting back into the spatial conservation law yields $ho  rac{d v^i}{dt} = -\partial_i p - ho \partial_i \Phi$, which is exactly the classical Euler equation where the gravitational force is $-ho 
abla \Phi$.
